content:
# Deuteronomy 18 — Disagreement Log
**SOPHIA Arbiter** | Sources: EMET (E), LOGOS (L), PNEUMA (P)

---

## v.2 — "as he spoke to him" (singular) vs. "as he promised them" (plural)

- **E:** "as he spoke to him" — singular *lo* preserved
- **L:** "as he promised them" — pluralizes
- **P:** "just as he promised them" — pluralizes
- **SOPHIA:** Retains singular but uses "promised" for clarity: "as he promised him." The MT has singular *lo*; L/P pluralize for contextual sense. SOPHIA preserves the MT singular.

---

## v.5 — "forever" vs. "all his days"

- **E:** "him and his sons, forever" (*le'olam*)
- **L:** "him and his sons, all his days"
- **P:** "them and their descendants for all generations"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "all his days" (L) as natural English for *le'olam* in this perpetual-office context. P's "all generations" is an interpretive expansion.

---

## v.6 — "with all the desire of his being/soul" — nephesh rendering

- **E:** "with all the desire of his being" (*avvat nafsho*)
- **L:** "whenever his soul desires"
- **P:** "comes in earnest desire"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "with all the desire of his soul" (blend E/L). P's "earnest desire" is dynamic but loses the *nephesh* (soul) reference.

---

## v.10 — "passes through the fire" vs. "burns as an offering" — addition of "as an offering"

- **E:** "makes his son or his daughter pass through the fire"
- **L:** "burns his son or his daughter as an offering" — adds "as an offering"
- **P:** "sacrifices their son or daughter in the fire" — renders as sacrifice
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts E's rendering "makes his son or his daughter pass through the fire" — closest to the MT *ma'avir ba-esh* (pass through the fire). L's "as an offering" and P's "sacrifices" are interpretive additions not in the MT; the precise nature of the rite is debated.

---

## v.10 — "practices divination" vs. "a diviner of divinations"

- **E:** "a diviner of divinations [qosem qesamim]" — preserves cognate intensifier
- **L:** "practices divination"
- **P:** "practices divination"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "practices divination" (L/P) for readability. E's cognate construction is noted as a textual observation.

---

## v.10 — "tells fortunes" (me'onen) vs. "soothsayer" vs. "reads omens"

- **E:** "a soothsayer [me'onen]"
- **L:** "tells fortunes"
- **P:** "reads omens"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "tells fortunes" (L). E's "soothsayer" is archaic; P's "reads omens" may overlap with the next item (*menachesh*).

---

## v.10 — "interprets omens" (menachesh) vs. "augur" vs. "interprets signs"

- **E:** "an augur [menachesh]"
- **L:** "interprets omens"
- **P:** "interprets signs"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "interprets omens" (L). E's "augur" is archaic.

---

## v.11 — "charmer" (chover chaver) vs. "casts a spell" vs. "casts spells"

- **E:** "a charmer [chover chaver]"
- **L:** "casts a spell"
- **P:** "casts spells"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "casts a spell" (L). E's "charmer" is archaic and loses the binding/knotting connotation of *chover chaver*.

---

## v.11 — "familiar spirit" (ov) / sho'el ov: "consults a medium" vs. "inquirer of a ghost"

- **E:** "one who inquires of a familiar spirit [sho'el ov]"
- **L:** "consults a medium"
- **P:** "consults a medium"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "consults a medium" (L/P). E's "familiar spirit" is technical but archaic; "medium" is the clearest English equivalent for the *ov* practitioner.

---

## v.11 — yidde'oni: "wizard" vs. "spiritist"

- **E:** "a wizard [yidde'oni]"
- **L:** "a spiritist"
- **P:** "a spiritist"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "a spiritist" (L/P). "Wizard" (E) carries connotations of fictional magic; "spiritist" preserves the Hebrew idea of someone with a "knowing spirit."

---

## v.13 — tamim: "wholehearted" vs. "blameless"

- **E:** "You shall be wholehearted [tamim] with the LORD your God"
- **L:** "You shall be blameless before the LORD your God"
- **P:** "You must be blameless before the LORD your God"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "You shall be wholehearted [tamim] before the LORD your God" (E with preposition from L). *Tamim* in this context calls for positive relational integrity, not merely the absence of fault. "Blameless" (L/P) leans negative; "wholehearted" captures the totality of covenant devotion contrasted with the divided loyalties of divination.

---

## v.13 — "with" vs. "before" the LORD

- **E:** "wholehearted with the LORD your God" (*im YHWH*)
- **L:** "blameless before the LORD your God"
- **P:** "blameless before the LORD your God"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "before" (L/P) as more natural English; the Hebrew *im* (with) implies coram Deo presence, which "before" captures.

---

## v.15 — word order and structure

- **E:** "A prophet from your midst, from your brothers, like me, the LORD your God will raise up for you—to him you shall listen."
- **L:** "The LORD your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among you, from your brothers — you shall listen to him —"
- **P:** "The LORD your God will raise up for you a prophet like me from among you — from your own people. Listen to him."
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts E's Hebrew word order with slight adjustment: "A prophet like me the LORD your God will raise up for you from your midst, from among your brothers—you shall listen to him." Fronting "A prophet like me" matches the Hebrew emphasis.

---

## v.17 — "They have done well" vs. "They are right" vs. "What they have said is good"

- **E:** "They have done well in what they have spoken"
- **L:** "They are right in what they have spoken"
- **P:** "What they have said is good"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "They have done well in what they have spoken" (E) as the most literal rendering of *heteyvu asher dibberu*.

---

## v.19 — "I myself will require it of him" vs. "I myself will call to account"

- **E:** "I myself will require [it] of him"
- **L:** "I myself will require it of him"
- **P:** "I myself will call to account anyone who does not listen"
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "I myself will require it of him" (E/L). P's "call to account" is accurate but weaker; the MT *anokhi edrosh* conveys direct divine accountability.

---

## v.22 — "you shall not be afraid of him" vs. "you need not be afraid"

- **E:** "you shall not be afraid of him"
- **L:** "You need not be afraid of him"
- **P:** "Have no fear of them" (shifts to plural)
- **SOPHIA:** Adopts "you need not be afraid of him" (L). E's imperative form is also acceptable; P's plural is inconsistent with the MT singular context.

---

*Total disagreements logged: 15*
